title: Tasting Science Guide
subtitle: Understanding wine through sensory science and chemistry

overview.title: The Art & Science of Tasting
overview.description: Wine tasting is a learnable skill, not an innate gift. Everyone's palate is different, and yours is not wrong. The goal is understanding and articulation, not finding correct answers.

philosophy.title: Our Philosophy
points: Wine tasting is a learnable skill, not an innate gift
Everyone's palate is different; yours is not wrong
The goal is understanding and articulation, not correct answers
Science explains but doesn't diminish the magic
Quality exists but so does subjectivity; both are true

the_winesaint_approach.title: The WineSaint Approach
the_winesaint_approach.description: Questions, not checklists. We believe in exploration over completion. Rather than mechanically working through a grid, ask yourself these questions:

questions.question: What do I see?
questions.purpose: Appearance tells a story before you smell anything

questions.question: What do I feel?
questions.purpose: Structure and texture on the palate

questions.question: How does it evolve?
questions.purpose: In the glass, on the palate, through the finish

why_science.title: Why Science Matters
why_science.content: Understanding why you perceive something makes knowledge sticky and transferable. When you know that the pepper note in Syrah comes from rotundone, you can recognize it in other contexts. When you understand that tannins bind to salivary proteins, you understand why steak softens a tannic wine. Science is not the enemy of poetry; it's the foundation that makes meaningful description possible.

sensory.title: Sensory Science
sensory.description: How we perceive wine through taste, smell, and touch. Understanding your sensory system is the foundation of tasting.

topics.name: The Five Basic Tastes
topics.content: Your tongue detects five basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami. In wine, we primarily encounter sweet (residual sugar), sour (acidity), and bitter (tannins, some phenolics). The old tongue map showing different regions for different tastes is a myth. All taste receptors are distributed across the tongue, though sensitivity varies slightly.

tastes.name: Umami
tastes.in_wine: Aged wines, lees contact, some aged Champagne

topics.name: Olfaction & Retronasal Smell
topics.content: 80% of what we call 'taste' is actually smell. You have approximately 400 types of olfactory receptors capable of detecting thousands of different volatile compounds. Wine contains 800+ aromatic compounds, though only a fraction exceed perception thresholds.

pathways.name: Orthonasal
pathways.description: Smelling through the nose, directly from the glass
pathways.what_it_tells_you: Primary assessment of aroma intensity, condition, complexity

pathways.name: Retronasal
pathways.description: Volatiles traveling from mouth to nasal cavity
pathways.when: While the wine is in your mouth and after swallowing
pathways.what_it_tells_you: Flavor confirmation, finish characteristics, additional complexity

topics.science: When you chew or warm wine in your mouth, volatile compounds vaporize and travel up the back of your throat to the olfactory epithelium. This is why wine 'tastes' different from how it smells.

topics.name: Trigeminal Sensations
topics.content: The trigeminal nerve detects physical and chemical irritation, giving us sensations that aren't taste or smell but are crucial to wine perception.

sensations.name: Heat/Burn
sensations.mechanism: TRPV1 receptor activation (same receptors as capsaicin)
sensations.perception: Warmth in throat and chest, especially in high-alcohol wines

sensations.name: Astringency
sensations.perception: Drying, gripping sensation; not a taste but a tactile feeling

sensations.name: Effervescence
sensations.mechanism: Carbonic acid formation, physical bubble burst

sensations.name: Cooling
sensations.perception: Rare in wine; some Rieslings have cool mineral sensation

topics.name: Perception Variability
topics.content: Everyone's palate is genuinely different. This isn't a cop-out; it's biology. Understanding your own sensory profile helps you communicate better and trust your perceptions.

factors.name: Supertasters
factors.description: ~25% of people have significantly more taste papillae
factors.impact: More sensitive to bitterness, may find tannic wines unpleasant

factors.name: Specific Anosmias
factors.impact: ~20% cannot smell rotundone (black pepper in Syrah)
factors.note: You may be 'blind' to certain aromas and not know it

factors.name: Threshold Differences
factors.impact: What's overwhelming to one person may be subtle to another
factors.note: Training can improve detection but not eliminate differences

factors.name: Age-Related Changes
factors.impact: Older tasters may prefer more aromatic wines
factors.note: Experience and vocabulary can compensate for reduced sensitivity

structure.title: Wine Structure
structure.description: The architectural elements that define how wine feels. Structure is the skeleton; flavor is the flesh.

elements.name: Acidity
elements.what_it_is: The sourness in wine from organic acids, primarily tartaric, malic, lactic, and citric. Acidity gives wine freshness, lift, and aging potential.

acids.name: Tartaric
acids.note: The primary acid in wine; doesn't degrade with ripening

acids.name: Malic
acids.note: Converted to lactic via MLF; higher in cool climates

acids.name: Lactic
acids.note: Result of MLF; gives buttery, softer mouthfeel

acids.name: Citric
acids.note: Present in small quantities; sometimes added for freshness

elements.how_to_detect: Acidity triggers salivation. The more your mouth waters, the higher the acid. Also creates a sensation of brightness, crispness, or sourness on the sides of your tongue.
elements.science: Acid activates sour taste receptors and triggers salivation as a protective/digestive response. pH typically ranges from 2.9 (very high acid) to 4.0 (very low acid).
elements.wine_impact: Freshness, aging potential, food pairing versatility, perception of sweetness (high acid makes wine seem drier)
elements.climate_correlation: Cool climates produce higher acid grapes; warm climates produce lower acid grapes

elements.name: Tannin
elements.what_it_is: Phenolic compounds from grape skins, seeds, stems, and oak. Tannins provide structure, texture, and aging potential, primarily in red wines.

sources.name: Seeds
sources.extraction: Released later in maceration; careful handling needed

sources.name: Oak
sources.extraction: From barrel aging; new oak contributes more

elements.how_to_detect: Astringency: the drying, gripping sensation on gums, tongue, and inner cheeks. Not a taste but a tactile sensation caused by proteins binding.
elements.science: Tannins are polyphenols that bind to salivary proteins, precipitating them and reducing lubrication. This creates the drying sensation. Over time, tannins polymerize and become softer.

spectrum.high: Firm, chewy, needs food or aging

elements.wine_impact: Mouthfeel, aging potential, food pairing (protein softens tannins)

examples.high: Nebbiolo, Cabernet Sauvignon, Tannat, young Barolo

elements.name: Alcohol
elements.what_it_is: Ethanol produced during fermentation. Alcohol contributes to body, texture, and perceived sweetness, but also heat.
elements.how_to_detect: Heat or warmth in the throat and chest. Higher alcohol wines feel 'hotter.' Also contributes to viscosity (legs/tears on glass).
elements.science: Ethanol activates TRPV1 receptors (the same receptors triggered by capsaicin in chili peppers), creating a burning sensation. It also lowers the boiling point of aromatic compounds, increasing their volatility.

levels.description: Very full, hot climate or fortified

elements.legs_myth: Legs (tears, wine fingers) indicate alcohol level and sugar, not quality. The Marangoni effect causes liquid with higher alcohol to sheet down the glass.
elements.wine_impact: Body, warmth, balance, perception of sweetness, aromatic intensity
elements.balance: Alcohol should be integrated, not searing. If you feel burn before flavor, the wine may be unbalanced.

elements.name: Body
elements.what_it_is: The overall weight and texture of wine in your mouth. Think of it like the difference between skim milk (light), whole milk (medium), and cream (full).
elements.how_to_detect: Weight on the palate. Does it feel light and refreshing or rich and coating? Compare to the milk analogy.
elements.wine_impact: Food pairing (match weight with food), style expectations, climate indication

elements.name: Sweetness
elements.what_it_is: Residual sugar (RS) remaining after fermentation. Most table wines are technically dry (less than 4 g/L RS), but perception of sweetness is affected by other factors.

spectrum.note: Below perception threshold for most people

elements.how_to_detect: Sweetness is perceived on the tip of the tongue as a viscous, coating sensation. But be careful: high acid can mask sweetness, and fruity aromas can suggest sweetness in dry wines.
perception_factors: High acidity makes wine seem drier than RS suggests
Fruity aromas can trick you into perceiving sweetness
elements.wine_impact: Style, food pairing, balance, aging (sweet wines age well due to sugar's preservative effect)

elements.name: Balance
elements.what_it_is: The harmony between structural elements. A balanced wine has no single element dominating uncomfortably. Balance is style-dependent: a tannic Barolo is balanced differently than a delicate Muscadet.
elements.how_to_assess: No element should stick out aggressively. Ask: Does the acidity feel appropriate? Is the tannin integrated? Does the alcohol show as heat? Is sweetness balanced by acid?
considerations: Balance is not the same as blandness. A wine can be high-acid, high-tannin, and high-alcohol yet still be balanced
Young wines may seem unbalanced but will integrate with age
Style matters: a sweet wine needs high acid for balance; a tannic wine needs fruit concentration
Balance is relative to the wine's category, not an absolute standard

examples.balance: High sugar balanced by high acid
High alcohol balanced by concentration and tannin
High tannin balanced by high acid and complex fruit
Light body balanced by crisp acidity

imbalance_signs: Acid that makes you wince without pleasure
Tannins that feel harsh without fruit to support them
Alcohol that burns before you taste anything
Sweetness that cloys without freshness to cut it

aromas.title: Aroma Compounds
aromas.description: The chemistry behind what you smell. Understanding where aromas come from helps you identify them and understand wine better.

primary.name: Primary Aromas
primary.description: Varietal character, terroir expression. These aromas are present in the grape and survive fermentation.
primary.examples: Citrus in Riesling, bell pepper in Cabernet Sauvignon, lychee in Gewurztraminer

secondary.name: Secondary Aromas
secondary.description: Yeast-derived aromas, malolactic fermentation byproducts, carbonic maceration characteristics.
secondary.examples: Butter from MLF, banana from carbonic maceration, bread dough from yeast

tertiary.name: Tertiary Aromas
tertiary.description: Oak influence, oxidative development, bottle bouquet. These develop over time.
tertiary.examples: Vanilla from oak, leather from bottle age, petrol from aged Riesling

compounds.name: Linalool
compounds.chemistry: Monoterpene alcohol; present in grape skins; increases with ripeness
compounds.note: One of the most important aromatic compounds in white wine

compounds.name: Rotundone
compounds.chemistry: Sesquiterpene; found in grape skins; also present in black peppercorns
compounds.note: Approximately 20% of the population cannot smell rotundone (specific anosmia)

compounds.name: Methoxypyrazines
compounds.aroma: Green bell pepper, grass, jalapeno, green pea
compounds.chemistry: Nitrogen-containing compounds; decrease with sun exposure and ripeness
compounds.note: Can be positive (herbaceous complexity) or negative (unripe, vegetal)

compounds.name: Volatile Thiols
compounds.chemistry: Sulfur-containing compounds released from odorless precursors during fermentation
compounds.note: Key to Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc character

compounds.name: Diacetyl
compounds.chemistry: Produced by lactic acid bacteria during MLF; can be metabolized further to less buttery compounds
compounds.note: Iconic Californian Chardonnay character; controversial (some love it, some hate it)

compounds.name: Fermentation Esters
compounds.chemistry: Formed from alcohol + acid; type depends on yeast strain and fermentation conditions

subtypes.name: Ethyl acetate
subtypes.aroma: Fruity, nail polish at high levels

compounds.note: Young wines are often ester-driven; these fade with age

compounds.name: Vanillin
compounds.chemistry: Released from oak lignin; more from new oak, American oak has higher levels than French
compounds.note: The most recognizable oak-derived aroma

compounds.name: Eugenol
found_in: Oak-aged wines, especially new French oak
compounds.chemistry: Extracted from oak; French oak typically has more than American
compounds.note: Part of the 'spice' character in oaked wines

compounds.name: Oak Lactones
compounds.chemistry: cis-isomer is more aromatic; American oak has 5-10x more than French
compounds.note: Why American oak gives that distinctive coconut note

compounds.name: TDN (Petrol)
compounds.chemistry: 1,1,6-trimethyl-1,2-dihydronaphthalene; forms from carotenoid precursors during aging
compounds.note: Considered a positive trait in aged Riesling; develops after 3-5+ years

compounds.name: Sotolon
compounds.chemistry: Forms during extended oxidative aging
compounds.note: The distinctive aroma of Vin Jaune and oxidized sweet wines

compounds.name: Brettanomyces Compounds
compounds.aroma: Band-aid, barnyard, horse stable, medicinal, smoky
compounds.chemistry: 4-ethylphenol and 4-ethylguaiacol produced by Brett from grape-derived precursors
compounds.note: Controversial: some consider low levels 'complexity,' others consider any level a fault

compounds.name: Geraniol
compounds.chemistry: Monoterpenol; highest in aromatic varieties; degrades to less floral compounds with age
compounds.note: One of the key aromatics distinguishing Gewurztraminer; name means 'crane' in Greek, related to geranium flower shape

compounds.name: Beta-Damascenone
compounds.aroma: Apple, rose, honey, quince, cooked apple
compounds.chemistry: Derived from carotenoid degradation; builds during grape ripening; remarkably potent
compounds.note: Despite low concentrations, contributes significantly to wine aroma due to incredibly low threshold. Enhances perception of fruitiness even below detection limit.

compounds.name: Beta-Ionone
compounds.chemistry: Carotenoid degradation product; responsible for violet notes in red wines
compounds.note: The classic violet note in Syrah and Nebbiolo comes primarily from beta-ionone

compounds.name: Eucalyptol (1,8-Cineole)
compounds.chemistry: Terpene found in eucalyptus trees; transfers to grapes via air/contact
compounds.note: Not grape-derived; comes from environmental eucalyptus. Once considered a 'New World' character, now understood as site-specific. Some argue it masks terroir.

compounds.name: Guaiacol (Smoke)
compounds.chemistry: From oak lignin degradation during toasting; also from wildfire smoke exposure
compounds.note: Positive in controlled amounts from barrel; fault when from wildfire smoke taint. Smoke taint releases more guaiacol during fermentation.

compounds.name: Furfural
compounds.chemistry: Formed from pentose sugar degradation during oak toasting
compounds.note: Contributes sweet, caramel, almond notes; higher in toasted vs air-dried oak

compounds.name: Acetaldehyde
compounds.chemistry: Intermediate in alcohol production; accumulates in oxidative conditions
compounds.note: Positive in Sherry, Vin Jaune; fault in fresh wines. First compound to increase during oxidation.

compounds.name: Citronellol
compounds.aroma: Citrus, rose, green, linalool-like but softer
compounds.chemistry: Monoterpenol; often formed from geraniol reduction during fermentation
compounds.note: Yeast can convert geraniol to citronellol during fermentation, changing the aromatic profile

compounds.name: 2-Phenylethanol
compounds.chemistry: Higher alcohol formed by yeast; levels influenced by fermentation temperature and nitrogen
compounds.note: Major contributor to floral notes in wine. Production increases at lower fermentation temperatures.

aroma_families.title: Aroma Family Groupings
aroma_families.description: Wine aromas can be organized into families to help systematic identification. Each family shares chemical or perceptual relationships.

families.examples: Lemon, apple, peach, pineapple, cherry, blackcurrant, raisin
Bell pepper, grass, eucalyptus, mint, tomato leaf
Vanilla, cinnamon, clove, black pepper, licorice
Forest floor, wet stone, truffle, must, chalk
Sherry, rotten eggs, nail polish, barnyard

faults.title: Wine Faults
faults.description: When something goes wrong. Recognizing faults is practical knowledge that helps you know when to send a wine back versus when to give it a chance.
faults.introduction: Wine faults exist on a spectrum. Some are clear-cut (corked wine), others are contextual (brett can be character or flaw). Learning to identify faults helps you distinguish 'I don't like this' from 'this is actually compromised.'

faults.name: Cork Taint (TCA)
faults.how_it_happens: TCA forms when chlorine compounds (from sanitizers) interact with fungi in cork. Can also occur in winery equipment, barrels, or even cardboard.
faults.detection: Wet cardboard, musty basement, damp newspaper, moldy cellar. Mutes fruit character, shortens finish.
faults.threshold: 1-2 parts per trillion (humans are extremely sensitive)
faults.severity: Wine is compromised at any detectable level
faults.what_to_do: Send it back. There's no saving a corked wine. Even if you can't smell it clearly, if the wine seems muted and flat, TCA may be to blame.
faults.note: Sometimes subtle. If a wine seems strangely dull or cardboard-like, suspect TCA even if the classic musty smell isn't obvious.

faults.name: Brettanomyces
faults.how_it_happens: Brett lives in wineries, especially in used barrels. Poor hygiene, low SO2, and high pH increase risk.
faults.detection: Band-aid, barnyard, horse stable, medicinal, sweaty saddle. At low levels: earthy, leathery, 'funky.'
faults.severity: Spectrum from subtle complexity to overwhelming flaw

controversy.defenders: Low levels add complexity, earthiness, terroir expression
controversy.critics: Any level masks varietal character and indicates poor winemaking
controversy.regional_tolerance: More accepted in traditional Rhone, some Burgundy; less in New World

faults.what_to_do: At low levels, decide if you find it appealing. At high levels (overpowering), it's a fault by any standard.

faults.name: Oxidation
faults.how_it_happens: Faulty closure, poor storage, damaged cork, or intentional winemaking (Sherry, Vin Jaune).

detection.visual: Browning in whites, orange/brick hues in reds
detection.aroma: Bruised apple, sherry-like nuttiness, dried fruit, flat
detection.palate: Loss of freshness, short finish, hollow

faults.chemistry: Acetaldehyde formation, polyphenol oxidation, color change from anthocyanin degradation
faults.what_to_do: If it's supposed to be fresh and isn't, it's faulted. If it's Sherry or Vin Jaune, enjoy.
faults.note: Premature oxidation ('premox') is a particular problem in white Burgundy.

faults.name: Volatile Acidity (VA)
faults.how_it_happens: Oxygen exposure allows bacteria to convert ethanol to acetic acid. Poor hygiene, stuck fermentation, or high-pH wines at risk.
faults.detection: Vinegar, nail polish remover. At low levels: lifted, slightly volatile, sharp.

threshold.legal: Varies by region; typically 1.2-1.4 g/L max

faults.severity: Low levels can add complexity and lift; high levels ruin wine

tolerance.traditional: Some traditional wines (old Barolo, natural wines) have higher VA
tolerance.modern: Most modern winemakers keep VA very low

faults.what_to_do: If it smells like salad dressing, it's too much. A slight volatile edge in aged wines can be normal.

faults.name: Reduction
faults.cause: Sulfur compounds from stressed yeast or lack of oxygen
faults.how_it_happens: Low nutrient fermentation, excessive SO2, or reductive winemaking gone too far. Opposite of oxidation.
faults.detection: Rotten eggs (H2S), struck match, rubber, garlic, cooked cabbage. At low levels: flinty, mineral, reductive.
faults.severity: Light reduction may dissipate; heavy reduction can persist
how_to_fix: Swirl aggressively to blow off H2S
Drop a clean copper coin in (binds to sulfur)
faults.note: Some winemakers use controlled reduction for freshness and ageability. If it clears with air, it's fine.

faults.name: Heat Damage (Cooked Wine)
faults.how_it_happens: Hot shipping, storage in warm environments, left in car trunk.

detection.visual: Pushed cork (wine expanded and leaked), sticky residue on capsule
detection.palate: Flat, lacking freshness, structure falls apart

faults.severity: Irreversible; accelerates all aging processes destructively
faults.prevention: Store wine in stable, cool conditions. Be wary of online purchases in summer.
faults.note: Check for seepage under the capsule. If there's sticky wine residue, suspect heat damage.

faults.name: Light Strike (Goût de Lumière)
faults.cause: UV light exposure degrading amino acids
faults.how_it_happens: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) absorbs UV, triggering reactions that produce sulfur compounds.
faults.detection: Skunky, wet wool, cooked cabbage, burnt rubber
faults.chemistry: UV + riboflavin leads to methionine and cysteine breakdown into sulfur compounds
faults.severity: Can develop within hours of sun exposure
faults.can_fix: May diminish slightly with aeration, but damage is mostly permanent
faults.prevention: Dark storage, colored glass bottles, UV-blocking glass
faults.note: This is why Champagne houses are now using UV-protective glass and why wine shops with bright lighting are concerning.

faults.name: Refermentation
faults.how_it_happens: Wine bottled with residual sugar and active yeast; more common in natural wines or winemaking errors.
faults.detection: Unexpected spritz/effervescence in still wine, haze, yeasty aromas
faults.severity: Minor spritz might be acceptable; heavy refermentation is a fault
faults.note: Some natural winemakers embrace this as 'pet-nat' character; in conventional wine, it's an error.

faults.name: Smoke Taint
faults.cause: Grape exposure to wildfire smoke during ripening
faults.how_it_happens: Volatile phenols (guaiacol, 4-methylguaiacol) from smoke are absorbed by grapes and bind to sugars as glycosides. During fermentation, these are released.

detection.aroma: Smoke, ash, ashtray, burnt plastic, bacon (in severe cases)
detection.palate: Ashy, bitter, dry finish. Often more apparent on the palate than nose
detection.delayed_release: Flavors intensify over time as glycoside-bound compounds are released

faults.chemistry: Smoke phenols exist in two forms: free volatiles (detectable) and glycoside-bound (released during fermentation, aging, and in the mouth). This is why wines can test clean but taste smoky.
faults.severity: Can render entire vintages undrinkable. No effective fix once in wine.
faults.prevention: Protective sprays show limited effectiveness. Early harvest before smoke exposure, or abandoning fruit entirely.
faults.economics: Smoke taint has caused billions in losses. Insurance claims, declassified wines, and abandoned harvests are increasingly common.
faults.what_to_do: Cannot be fixed. Wines with smoke taint should not be purchased for aging; some low-level tainted wines may be acceptable for immediate consumption.

faults.name: Mouse Taint (Mousiness)
faults.how_it_happens: Microorganisms produce tetrahydropyridines from lysine and other amino acids. Often associated with low-sulfur winemaking.

detection.initial: Often undetectable on first sniff or sip
detection.delayed: Becomes apparent 15-30 seconds after tasting as saliva raises pH
detection.character: Mouse cage, crackers, popcorn, stale cereal, urine-like

faults.why_delayed: The compounds (2-acetyl-1-pyrroline, 2-acetyltetrahydropyridine) are pH-sensitive. They're not volatile at wine pH (~3.5) but become detectable as saliva raises mouth pH toward neutral.
faults.severity: Once developed, permanent. Highly divisive - some cannot detect it at all.
faults.sensitivity: About 30% of people cannot perceive mouse taint even at high levels. This makes it controversial in natural wine circles.
faults.prevalence: More common in low-sulfur, natural wines due to reduced antimicrobial protection
faults.what_to_do: There is no fix. If you detect it, don't assume others will or won't. Natural wine advocates debate whether low levels are acceptable.

faults.name: Ladybug Taint (IPMP)
faults.cause: Asian lady beetles (Harmonia axyridis) processed with grapes
faults.how_it_happens: Stressed lady beetles release a defensive compound (2-isopropyl-3-methoxypyrazine, IPMP) that persists through fermentation.
faults.detection: Peanut shells, peanut butter, musty, green, vegetal at extreme levels
faults.chemistry: IPMP is chemically similar to IBMP (bell pepper pyrazine) but with a distinctive nutty character. Even a few beetles per ton can cause detectable taint.
faults.severity: Can be overwhelming above threshold; less severe than cork taint
faults.can_fix: Partially - some fining agents can reduce levels
faults.prevention: Vineyard management to reduce beetle populations, optical sorters, hand-sorting
faults.what_to_do: If overwhelming, the wine is compromised. Low levels may be tolerable to some.

faults.name: Geranium Taint
faults.cause: Sorbic acid breakdown by lactic acid bacteria
faults.how_it_happens: Sorbic acid (preservative) is metabolized to 2-ethoxyhexa-3,5-diene, which smells like crushed geranium leaves.
faults.detection: Strong crushed geranium leaf aroma, medicinal, artificial floral
faults.severity: Ruins wine at any detectable level
faults.prevention: Don't use sorbic acid in wines that will undergo MLF, or ensure MLF is complete and SO2 is adequate before using sorbic acid
faults.note: More common in sweet wines where sorbic acid is used to prevent refermentation. Essentially a winemaking error.

faults.name: Filter Pad/Paper Taint
faults.cause: Contaminated filter pads or paper products
faults.how_it_happens: Chlorophenols or TCA can form in cardboard, paper, and filter materials, similar to cork taint.
faults.detection: Same as cork taint: wet cardboard, musty, muted fruit
faults.chemistry: TCA and its relatives can form anywhere chlorine and fungi interact - including filter pads, cardboard cases, and winery wood
faults.severity: Same as cork taint - compromises wine
faults.note: Some wineries have discovered their entire production contaminated by TCA in winery infrastructure, not corks

faults.name: Hydrogen Sulfide (H2S)
faults.how_it_happens: When yeast lacks adequate nitrogen nutrients or sulfate levels are high, they produce H2S as a metabolic byproduct. Can also come from elemental sulfur sprays applied too close to harvest.
faults.chemistry: H2S itself is volatile and can be removed by aeration. If not addressed, it converts to more stable mercaptans (garlic, onion) and disulfides (burnt rubber, sewage) that are much harder to fix.
faults.severity: Early H2S is fixable; mercaptans and disulfides are harder or impossible to remove
how_to_fix: For consumers: decant with vigorous splash, drop clean copper coin
faults.note: H2S in bottle is often from reductive winemaking choices; may blow off entirely with aeration

fault_spectrum.title: The Fault vs. Feature Spectrum
fault_spectrum.description: Not all wine faults are clear-cut. Some sit on a spectrum where low levels may add complexity while high levels ruin the wine. Context, intent, and consumer tolerance all matter.

spectrum.example: Old-school Rhône, some traditional Burgundy may have detectable brett

spectrum.clearly_fault: High levels (>1.2 g/L) - vinegar, nail polish
spectrum.example: Some old Barolo, natural wines, intentional in some orange wines

spectrum.clearly_fault: Persistent garlic, onion, sewage after aeration
spectrum.controversial: Flinty, struck match notes - may be terroir expression
Existence itself is debated - not all can taste it
spectrum.example: Ongoing debate in natural wine circles

spectrum.clearly_fault: In wines meant to be fresh - Sherry character in Chablis
spectrum.controversial: Intentional in Sherry, Madeira, Vin Jaune, some orange wines
spectrum.example: Premox in white Burgundy vs intentional oxidative aging

quality.title: Quality Assessment
quality.description: How to evaluate wine beyond personal preference. Quality exists, but so does subjectivity. Learning to distinguish them is part of developing a palate.

introduction.title: The Subjectivity Problem
introduction.content: Quality is not purely objective. But it's not purely subjective either. There are defensible criteria that experts largely agree on, even if individual preferences vary. Learning to apply these criteria while acknowledging your biases is the goal.

markers.name: Complexity
markers.definition: Multiple layers of aroma and flavor that evolve over time, both in the glass and on the palate.
markers.how_to_assess: Does the wine reveal new elements as it opens up? Does it change in the glass? Do you notice different aromas and flavors as it evolves?
markers.examples: A simple wine might smell like 'cherry.' A complex wine might show cherry, then earth, then spice, then something floral, then something savory as it opens.
markers.note: Simple does not equal bad. Some wines are meant to be straightforward and refreshing. Complexity is one quality marker, not the only one.

markers.name: Concentration
markers.definition: Intensity of flavor without heaviness. The sense that there's substantial fruit and extract without being soupy or overripe.
markers.how_to_assess: Is there depth and persistence to the fruit? Does the wine have presence on the palate without being heavy or jammy?
markers.note: Concentration is about focus and definition, not volume. A concentrated Burgundy is very different from a concentrated Amarone, but both have intensity appropriate to their style.

markers.name: Length (Finish)
markers.definition: How long positive flavors persist after swallowing or spitting. The wine's 'echo.'
markers.how_to_assess: After swallowing, count seconds. Basic wines: 3-5 seconds. Good wines: 6-10 seconds. Outstanding wines: 15+ seconds.
markers.what_counts: Positive flavor persistence. Bitter, harsh, or hot aftertastes don't count as 'length.'
caveats: More tannic wines can seem to have longer finish due to astringency
Higher acid wines tend to have longer perceived finish
Sweet wines often have very long finish
markers.note: Length correlates strongly with quality across all wine styles.

markers.name: Balance
markers.definition: Harmony between structural elements. No single component dominates uncomfortably.
markers.how_to_assess: Does any element stick out aggressively? Does the acidity, tannin, alcohol, or sweetness feel integrated?
examples: A high-acid Champagne is balanced because the bubbles and dosage integrate it
A high-alcohol Amarone is balanced because the concentration matches
A young, tannic Barolo may seem unbalanced but will integrate with age
markers.note: Balance must be assessed relative to style expectations and age trajectory.

markers.name: Typicity
markers.definition: Does the wine express its origin and variety? Does Chablis taste like Chablis? Does Pinot taste like Pinot?
markers.how_to_assess: Compare to benchmark examples. Does the wine meet style expectations for its region and variety?

controversy.traditionalists: Typicity is essential; wine should taste of its place
controversy.modernists: Innovation and personal expression matter more than conformity

markers.note: Typicity is valued in some contexts (classic regions) and less relevant in others (new wine regions, experimental wines).

quality_vs_preference.title: Quality vs. Preference
quality_vs_preference.content: A wine can be high quality and not to your taste. Both truths coexist. Learning to distinguish 'I don't like this' from 'this is flawed' is part of developing a palate. You might recognize that a wine is well-made while not personally enjoying it. Conversely, you might love a wine that's technically simple. Both responses are valid.

rating_systems.title: A Note on Ratings
rating_systems.content: Point scores (100-point, 20-point) are imperfect tools that compress multidimensional experiences into single numbers. They're useful for rough comparison but can be misleading. A 92-point wine isn't objectively 'better' than an 88-point wine; it's just one taster's assessment. Use ratings as guides, not verdicts.

perception.title: Perception Factors
perception.description: Everything that affects your experience beyond the wine itself. Understanding these variables helps you taste more accurately and enjoy wine more fully.

factors.name: Temperature
factors.impact: Temperature dramatically affects wine perception. Cold mutes aromas and emphasizes acid and tannin. Warm amplifies aromas but also alcohol.
factors.science: Temperature affects volatile compound release (warmer = more aromatics) and receptor sensitivity (cold numbs taste buds slightly).

guidelines.reason: Opens aromatics; don't serve too warm

common_mistakes: Whites too cold: Mutes all aromatics; you taste only acid
Reds too warm: Alcohol dominates; wine tastes flabby and hot
factors.tip: When in doubt, serve slightly cooler than you think. The wine will warm in the glass.

factors.name: Glassware
factors.impact: Glass shape affects how aromas concentrate and where wine hits your palate. But the importance is often overstated by marketing.

what_matters.effect: Larger bowls allow more aeration and aroma development
Narrower concentrates aromas; wider disperses them
Thinner rims feel more elegant; minor impact on flavor
Keeps hand warmth away from wine; allows swirling

factors.recommendation: One good all-purpose wine glass covers 80% of needs. A larger bowl for reds, a smaller for whites, and a flute for sparkling covers everything else.
factors.minimum_requirements: Clear glass, stem, reasonably thin rim, some bowl curvature to concentrate aromas. That's it.

factors.name: Decanting
factors.impact: Decanting introduces oxygen, which can soften tannins, release aromas, and 'wake up' a closed wine. Also removes sediment.
factors.science: Oxygen promotes oxidation (in small amounts, beneficial), volatile release, and tannin polymerization (softening).
when_helpful: Wines with sediment (old reds, vintage Port)
when_harmful: Old, fragile wines (may fall apart with air)
factors.double_decanting: Decant into a vessel, then back into the rinsed bottle. Combines aeration with elegant service.

factors.name: Food Pairing
factors.impact: Food and wine interact chemically and perceptually. The right pairing enhances both; the wrong pairing diminishes both.

science.effect: Tannins bind to proteins, softening their astringency. Steak makes tannic wine seem smoother.
Acid cuts through richness, refreshing the palate. Champagne with fried food works.
Salt reduces bitterness perception. Salty cheese makes tannic wine more approachable.
Sugar tames heat. Off-dry Riesling with spicy food.

principles: Match weight: Light wine with light food, full wine with rich food
Bridge flavors: Earthy wine with earthy food, fruity wine with fruity sauces
Contrast: Acid or bubbles cut through richness
Sweetness: Wine should be at least as sweet as the food
danger_zones: Artichokes (contain cynarin, makes wine taste sweet)
Asparagus (sulfur compounds clash with many wines)
Very spicy food (overwhelms most wines; go off-dry)
Chocolate with dry red wine (usually clashes)

factors.name: Expectation Bias
factors.impact: What you expect to taste changes what you actually taste. Price, label, context, and prior knowledge all influence perception measurably.

research.finding: Wine experts described a white wine dyed red using red wine descriptors
Brain activity differs when tasting identical wine labeled at different prices
Experts used red wine vocabulary for white wine when colored red

factors.how_it_works: Your brain uses context as a prior when interpreting sensory data. If you expect something to be good (expensive, famous, recommended), you're primed to perceive it positively.
implications: Taste blind when possible for honest assessment
Be aware that marketing influences you
Price does not reliably indicate quality beyond a threshold
Famous names carry weight that may not be deserved
factors.takeaway: You cannot be fully objective. The best you can do is acknowledge your biases and taste blind when accurate assessment matters.

factors.name: Sensory Adaptation
factors.impact: Your senses adapt quickly to continuous stimulation. The second sip of wine tastes different from the first.
factors.science: Receptors fatigue with constant stimulation, reducing sensitivity. This is why perfume fades after you've worn it a while.
in_wine_tasting: First sip: Most impactful; trust initial impressions
Subsequent sips: Adapted; better for structural assessment
Palate fatigue: After many wines, sensitivity decreases
how_to_manage: Cleanse palate with water, bread, or neutral crackers between wines
Take breaks when tasting many wines
Trust first impressions for character; use subsequent sips for analysis
Smell coffee beans to 'reset' olfactory receptors (somewhat effective)
factors.note: This is why the first taste at a restaurant is important; your palate is fresh and able to detect faults.

appearance.title: Visual Assessment
appearance.description: Before you smell or taste, wine tells a story through its appearance. Color, clarity, and viscosity provide clues about age, grape variety, climate, and winemaking.

clarity.title: Clarity
clarity.description: How clear or hazy the wine appears when held against light.

levels.description: Crystal clear, highly reflective, no particles

levels.indicates: Light or no filtration, possible colloid instability
Unfiltered wine, may be intentional (natural wine) or fault

levels.description: Cannot see through the wine, significant particles
levels.indicates: Unfiltered, possibly refermented, or heavily tannic

clarity.context: Haze is expected in unfiltered natural wines but may indicate problems in conventionally made wines.

color_white.title: White Wine Color
color_white.description: White wines darken with age and oak exposure.

progression.indicates: Very young, cool climate, stainless steel

progression.examples: Oaked Chardonnay, Sauternes, older white Burgundy

progression.indicates: Significant age, oxidative winemaking, or sweet wine
progression.examples: Aged Sauternes, Vin Jaune, very old white Burgundy
Old Sherry, Madeira, severely oxidized wine

special_cases.cause: Very brief skin contact, rosé-like treatment

color_red.title: Red Wine Color
color_red.description: Red wines lighten with age, shifting from purple toward brick and tawny.

progression.examples: Aged Nebbiolo, old Bordeaux, aged Pinot Noir

rim_assessment.title: The Rim
rim_assessment.description: Tilt the glass at 45° over white paper. The rim (meniscus) shows age:
observations: Young wines: Core and rim similar color
Developing wines: Garnet or orange rim with ruby core
Mature wines: Wide orange/tawny rim, fading core
Old wines: Rim may be watery, core brownish

variety_clues.color: Deep ruby to purple, moderate aging

viscosity.title: Viscosity & Legs
viscosity.description: The 'legs' or 'tears' that form on the glass after swirling. Often misunderstood as a quality indicator.

science.name: Marangoni Effect
science.explanation: As alcohol evaporates from the thin film on the glass, surface tension increases, pulling wine upward. Droplets form and run down as 'legs.'
science.what_it_indicates: Alcohol and/or sugar content - NOT quality

viscosity.myth_busting: Legs indicate alcohol and sugar, not quality, concentration, or terroir. A cheap high-alcohol wine has impressive legs; a refined cool-climate Burgundy may have minimal legs. Ignore them as quality signals.

professional_methods.title: Professional Tasting Frameworks
professional_methods.description: Several standardized tasting methodologies exist for wine education and professional evaluation. Each has strengths and limitations.

methods.name: WSET Systematic Approach to Tasting (SAT)
limitations: Quality assessment criteria can be reductive
Some find it stifles personal expression

methods.name: Court of Master Sommeliers Deductive Method
methods.purpose: Designed to identify wines blind - what, where, when
strengths: Excellent for developing blind tasting skills
limitations: Focused on identification, less on enjoyment
Old World/New World binary increasingly outdated

methods.name: 20-Point Scale (Traditional European)
methods.description: The older European standard for wine evaluation, still used in some contexts.

methods.name: 100-Point Scale (Parker/American)
methods.description: Dominant consumer-facing scoring system, criticized and defended in equal measure.
defense: Provides consumer guidance in vast market
Better than no guidance at all

blind_tasting.title: Blind Tasting Techniques
blind_tasting.description: Tasting without knowing the wine's identity removes bias and tests true perceptual skills.

levels.definition: You know what wines are being served, but not which is which
You don't know what wines are being served or in what order

strategies.explanation: High acid + high tannin = cool climate or specific varieties. High alcohol + low acid = warm climate.
Before analysis, what's your gut impression? Fruity? Earthy? Green? This often points to variety or region.
strategies.practice: Train yourself to capture first impression quickly

strategies.explanation: Start with broad categories (Old vs New World), then narrow (country, region, variety, vintage).
strategies.practice: Build a decision tree you can follow under pressure

strategies.explanation: What this wine is NOT can be as useful as what it IS.
Aroma descriptors are personal and inconsistent. Acid, tannin, and alcohol are more objective.
strategies.practice: Calibrate your structural perception with measured wines

common_traps: Guessing too quickly before adequate assessment
Letting one feature dominate your conclusion
Anchoring on first guess and seeking confirming evidence
Mistaking personal preference for objective assessment

service.title: Wine Service
service.description: How wine is served affects your experience as much as the wine itself. Temperature, glassware, and timing all matter.

steps: Insert worm off-center, twist clockwise keeping worm straight
Insert until one spiral remains visible
Lever against first notch, lift partially
Lever against second notch, complete extraction
Wipe rim, present cork to host if in service context
Use a two-pronged cork puller (ah-so/butler's friend)
Insert longer prong first, rock back and forth
Work both prongs down sides of cork
If cork crumbles, use a port tongs or push remaining cork in
Chill wine to 6-8°C - cold wine releases less gas
Remove foil and wire cage while keeping thumb on cork
Hold cork firmly, twist BOTTLE (not cork)
Ease out cork with gentle resistance - quiet sigh, not pop
Hold bottle at 45° after opening to increase surface area
Pour gently down side of tilted glass
techniques.safety: A warm champagne cork can launch at 50 km/h. Always point away from people and valuables.

order.title: Tasting Order

principles.reasoning: Delicate wines are overwhelmed after heavy ones
Sweetness makes subsequent wines taste sour
Aged wines are more delicate and can be overwhelmed
Lighter wines first, though exceptions exist (e.g., sweet white last)
Palate fatigue reduces appreciation of nuance

exceptions: Great aged Champagne may come after white Burgundy
Sweet white may conclude after red
A flight of one grape/region may progress any direction
Palate cleansers (water, bread) reset between dramatically different wines

factors.reasoning: Palate is fresh but not empty; alertness is high

factors.optimal: Light meal 1-2 hours before; not immediately after heavy/spicy food
factors.reasoning: Empty stomach distorts perception; full stomach dulls it
Competes with wine aromas; in professional settings, often prohibited

factors.optimal: Clean but not freshly brushed; no coffee, gum, or mints within 30 minutes
factors.reasoning: Mint and toothpaste particularly distort wine perception

factors.optimal: Well-lit (natural light ideal), neutral smells, white backgrounds for color
factors.reasoning: Light affects color perception; odors compete with wine

factors.optimal: Maximum 15-20 wines per session with breaks
factors.reasoning: Sensory adaptation diminishes discrimination after extended tasting
